name: India
tagline: From the planned cities of the Indus to the world's largest democracy.
summary: India's recorded story stretches back more than four and a half thousand years to the Bronze Age cities of the Indus Valley, making it one of the oldest continuous civilisations on Earth. Across the millennia it gave birth to four world religions, the Sanskrit classics, the decimal numeral system and a succession of empires from the Maurya to the Gupta and the Mughals. After roughly two centuries of British commercial and then Crown rule, India won its independence on 15 August 1947 amid the trauma of Partition, and on 26 January 1950 adopted a constitution that established it as a sovereign democratic republic.

founding.label: Independence from Britain
founding.detail: India gained independence from British rule on 15 August 1947, ending nearly two centuries of East India Company and Crown dominance. It became a sovereign democratic republic when its constitution came into force on 26 January 1950, now celebrated as Republic Day. Yet India's civilisational roots run far deeper, to the urban Indus Valley Civilisation that flourished from around 2600 BCE.

eras.title: The Indus Valley Civilisation
eras.period: c. 2600 – c. 1900 BCE (roots to c. 3300 BCE)
eras.standfirst: Along the Indus and its tributaries arose one of the world's earliest urban civilisations, with planned cities, standardised weights and a script still undeciphered.
body: The Indus Valley (or Harappan) Civilisation was a Bronze Age society spanning the north-western Indian subcontinent. Its mature, urban phase is usually dated to roughly 2600–1900 BCE, though earlier village cultures in the region reach back to around 3300 BCE and beyond. At its height it was contemporary with, and comparable in scale to, ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia.

Its great cities — Harappa and Mohenjo-daro chief among them, both flourishing around 2600 BCE and each home to tens of thousands of people — display a remarkable command of urban planning: gridded streets, baked-brick houses, sophisticated drainage and covered sewers, public baths and large granaries. Standardised weights and measures and a uniform material culture spread across hundreds of settlements, pointing to extensive trade and shared administration.

The Harappans left a script of short inscriptions on seals that has never been deciphered, so their language, beliefs and rulers remain largely unknown. From around 1900 BCE the cities declined and were gradually abandoned, a shift now linked chiefly to climate change and the weakening of monsoon-fed rivers rather than any single invasion.

events.title: Mature urban phase of the Indus cities
events.summary: Harappa, Mohenjo-daro and hundreds of other settlements reach their peak, with grid-planned streets, advanced drainage and standardised weights across a vast trading network.

events.title: Mohenjo-daro flourishes
events.summary: Mohenjo-daro, on the Indus in modern Pakistan, becomes one of the civilisation's largest cities and is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site renowned for its Great Bath and citadel.

events.title: Decline of the Harappan cities
events.summary: The major cities are gradually abandoned as weakening monsoons and shifting rivers undermine the agricultural base, marking the end of the civilisation's urban phase.

eras.title: The Vedic Age and the Great Religions
eras.standfirst: Out of the post-Harappan world came the Vedas, the Sanskrit language and the spiritual ferment that produced Hinduism, Buddhism and Jainism.
body: Following the decline of the Indus cities, Indo-Aryan-speaking peoples and their culture spread across northern India, ushering in the Vedic period of roughly 1500–500 BCE. The Rigveda, the oldest of the four Vedas and one of the world's earliest religious texts, was composed in archaic Sanskrit during the earlier part of this age and transmitted orally for centuries.

Vedic society organised itself into kingdoms and lineages, developed the ritual and philosophical traditions that would crystallise into Hinduism, and produced the Upanishads, whose speculation on the self and ultimate reality reshaped Indian thought from around 500 BCE.

The mid-first millennium BCE was a time of extraordinary spiritual creativity. In the Gangetic plain, Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha, taught a path beyond suffering, founding Buddhism, while his near-contemporary Mahavira reformed and propagated Jainism. Both movements rejected aspects of Vedic ritual and caste authority and would profoundly shape India and, in Buddhism's case, much of Asia.

events.title: Composition of the Rigveda
events.summary: The Rigveda, the oldest Vedic text and a foundational scripture of Hinduism, is composed in early Sanskrit and preserved through oral tradition.

events.title: Rise of the Buddha and Mahavira
events.summary: Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) founds Buddhism and Mahavira reforms Jainism, launching two enduring religions amid a wider questioning of Vedic ritual.

figures.name: Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha)
figures.blurb: A prince of the Shakya clan who renounced worldly life and, after attaining enlightenment, taught the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path. His teachings spread from India across Asia to become one of the world's major religions.

eras.title: Classical Empires: Maurya and the Gupta Golden Age
eras.standfirst: India's first great pan-Indian empire under the Mauryas gave way, centuries later, to the Gupta era, remembered as a classical golden age of art, science and Sanskrit literature.
body: Around 322 BCE Chandragupta Maurya founded the Maurya Empire, the first state to unite most of the Indian subcontinent under a single administration. Under his grandson Ashoka (reigned c. 268–232 BCE) the empire reached its greatest extent. After the bloody conquest of Kalinga, Ashoka embraced Buddhism, renounced aggressive war, and propagated principles of dharma through edicts carved on pillars and rocks across his realm.

After the Mauryas fragmented in the early second century BCE, India saw a succession of regional powers until the Gupta dynasty established a new empire over much of northern India in the fourth century CE. The imperial Gupta period, roughly 320–550 CE, is widely remembered as a classical golden age, though some scholars caution against idealising it.

Under rulers such as Chandragupta I, Samudragupta and Chandragupta II 'Vikramaditya', Sanskrit literature, sculpture, temple architecture and learning flourished. The poet and dramatist Kalidasa produced enduring masterpieces, the mathematician-astronomer Aryabhata advanced the science of numbers and the cosmos, and Indian mathematicians developed the place-value decimal system and the concept of zero that would transform global mathematics. The empire declined in the sixth century under dynastic strife and Huna invasions.

events.title: Chandragupta Maurya founds the Maurya Empire
events.summary: Chandragupta Maurya overthrows the Nanda dynasty and builds the first empire to unify most of the subcontinent, with its capital at Pataliputra.

events.title: Ashoka's conquest of Kalinga and conversion
events.summary: After the devastating Kalinga war, Emperor Ashoka embraces Buddhism, renounces conquest, and spreads an ethic of dharma through inscribed edicts across his vast empire.

events.title: Gupta golden age
events.summary: The Gupta Empire presides over a classical flowering of Sanskrit literature, mathematics and astronomy, including the works of Kalidasa and Aryabhata and the maturing of the decimal system.

figures.name: Ashoka
figures.blurb: The greatest Maurya ruler, who after the carnage of the Kalinga war turned to Buddhism and non-violence, promoting moral governance through rock and pillar edicts. His lion capital is today the national emblem of India.

figures.name: Aryabhata
figures.blurb: A Gupta-era scholar who worked with positional notation, accurately approximated pi, and proposed that the Earth rotates on its axis, embodying the scientific achievements of classical India.

pullquote.text: All men are my children. As for my own children I desire that they may be provided with all the welfare and happiness of this world and of the next, so do I desire for all men.

eras.title: The Delhi Sultanate and the Mughal Empire
eras.standfirst: From 1206 a series of Muslim dynasties ruled from Delhi, culminating in the Mughal Empire, which fused Persian, Islamic and Indian cultures into one of the wealthiest realms of its age.
body: In 1206 Qutb al-Din Aibak, a former general, established independent rule that became the Delhi Sultanate, a Persianate Islamic state that over three centuries was ruled in turn by five dynasties — the Mamluk, Khalji, Tughlaq, Sayyid and Lodi lines. The Sultanate at its height dominated much of the subcontinent and left a deep imprint on its architecture, language and administration.

In 1526 Babur, a Central Asian prince descended from Timur and Genghis Khan, defeated the last Lodi sultan at the First Battle of Panipat and founded the Mughal Empire. Under his grandson Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) the empire was consolidated through able administration and a policy of religious accommodation that drew Hindus and Muslims into a shared imperial order.

The Mughals presided over a brilliant synthesis of Persian and Indian culture, producing masterpieces of painting, poetry and architecture, above all the Taj Mahal built by Shah Jahan. By the reign of Aurangzeb (1658–1707) the empire reached its greatest territorial extent, but costly wars in the Deccan, heavy taxation and religious friction strained it. After Aurangzeb's death in 1707 imperial power fragmented, opening the way for regional states and, ultimately, European intrusion.

events.title: Founding of the Delhi Sultanate
events.summary: Qutb al-Din Aibak establishes independent rule at Delhi, beginning a 320-year succession of Islamic dynasties that reshape the politics and culture of northern India.

events.title: Babur founds the Mughal Empire
events.summary: Babur defeats Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat, ending the Delhi Sultanate and establishing the Mughal Empire, which would rule much of the subcontinent for over two centuries.

events.title: Reign of Akbar
events.summary: Akbar the Great consolidates and expands the empire, reforms its administration with the mansabdari system, and pursues a policy of religious tolerance and cultural synthesis.

events.title: Death of Aurangzeb and Mughal decline
events.summary: Aurangzeb dies after extending the empire to its greatest extent; his costly wars and religious policies leave the Mughal state weakened, and its authority rapidly fragments thereafter.

figures.name: Akbar
figures.blurb: The third and arguably greatest Mughal emperor, who consolidated the empire across northern and central India, built an efficient administration, and promoted dialogue among India's religions.

eras.title: The East India Company and the British Raj
eras.standfirst: A trading company became the master of a subcontinent, and after the rebellion of 1857 the British Crown ruled India directly until the rising tide of nationalism forced its withdrawal.
body: The British East India Company's victory at the Battle of Plassey in 1757, won by Robert Clive over the Nawab of Bengal, transformed a commercial enterprise into a territorial power. Over the following century the Company expanded across India through war, treaty and annexation, draining wealth from the subcontinent while imposing its own administration.

Resentment at Company rule erupted in the Indian Rebellion of 1857, a vast uprising that shook British power. After it was suppressed, the British Crown abolished Company rule in 1858 and took direct control, inaugurating the British Raj, which would govern India until 1947. The Raj built railways and a unified administration, but its rule was extractive and its priorities imperial.

Colonial policy bore a terrible human cost. India suffered repeated famines under British rule, culminating in the Bengal famine of 1943, in which an estimated two to three million people died — a catastrophe widely attributed not to crop failure but to wartime policy, inflation, export priorities and administrative neglect.

Against this backdrop a powerful nationalist movement grew, led by the Indian National Congress and increasingly by Mahatma Gandhi, whose mass campaigns of non-violent resistance — the Salt March of 1930, the Quit India movement of 1942 — mobilised millions and made continued British rule untenable.

events.title: Battle of Plassey
events.summary: Robert Clive's East India Company forces defeat the Nawab of Bengal, marking the beginning of British political and military dominance in India.

events.title: British Crown rule begins
events.summary: After the suppression of the 1857 rebellion, the Crown abolishes East India Company rule and assumes direct governance of India, beginning the British Raj.

events.title: The Salt March
events.summary: Gandhi leads a 24-day march to the sea to make salt in defiance of the British salt monopoly, galvanising mass non-violent resistance against colonial rule.

events.title: Bengal famine
events.summary: An estimated two to three million people die in Bengal in a man-made famine driven by wartime inflation, export priorities and colonial policy rather than any major shortfall in food production.

figures.name: Mohandas (Mahatma) Gandhi
figures.blurb: The pre-eminent leader of Indian nationalism, who developed satyagraha — non-violent civil resistance — and led mass campaigns such as the Salt March and Quit India that mobilised the country against British rule.

pullquote.text: The famine was the result not of a shortage of food but of a failure of entitlement.
pullquote.attribution: Paraphrasing economist Amartya Sen on the Bengal famine of 1943

eras.title: Independence, Partition and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: Freedom in 1947 came with the agony of Partition; three years later India became a republic, and has since grown into the world's most populous democracy.
body: At midnight on 15 August 1947 India became independent, with Jawaharlal Nehru as its first prime minister. Independence was inseparable from Partition: the subcontinent was divided into India and Pakistan along the hastily drawn Radcliffe Line, splitting Punjab and Bengal and triggering one of the largest forced migrations in history.

Partition was accompanied by catastrophic violence. Between roughly 12 and 20 million people were displaced across the new borders, and estimates of the dead range widely, commonly cited at around one to two million. Communities that had lived together for generations were torn apart by massacres, mass abductions and sexual violence — a trauma that still shadows relations between India and Pakistan.

On 26 January 1950 the Constitution of India came into force, transforming the newly independent dominion into a sovereign democratic republic. Drafted under the chairmanship of B. R. Ambedkar, it established universal adult suffrage, fundamental rights and a federal parliamentary system, and abolished untouchability. The date is celebrated each year as Republic Day.

In the decades since, India has held regular multiparty elections, built a diverse industrial and technological economy, and become the most populous nation on Earth and the world's largest democracy — while continuing to grapple with inequality, communal tension and the unfinished work of inclusion.

events.title: Independence and Partition
events.summary: India becomes independent from Britain under Prime Minister Nehru as the subcontinent is partitioned into India and Pakistan, unleashing mass migration and communal violence.

events.title: Partition violence and mass migration
events.summary: Up to 12–20 million people are uprooted and an estimated one to two million die in massacres and forced migration along the new India–Pakistan border.

events.title: Constitution and the Republic
events.summary: The Constitution of India comes into force, making India a sovereign democratic republic with universal suffrage and fundamental rights; the day is celebrated as Republic Day.

figures.name: Jawaharlal Nehru
figures.blurb: A leading figure of the independence movement and India's first prime minister, who shaped the new republic's democratic institutions, secular constitution and non-aligned foreign policy.

figures.name: B. R. Ambedkar
figures.blurb: A jurist, economist and social reformer who chaired the drafting committee of the Constitution and championed the rights of the Dalits and other marginalised communities.

pullquote.text: At the stroke of the midnight hour, when the world sleeps, India will awake to life and freedom.

figures.name: Ashoka
figures.blurb: Greatest of the Maurya emperors, who after the Kalinga war embraced Buddhism and non-violence and propagated dharma through inscribed edicts.

figures.name: Akbar
figures.blurb: Consolidator of the Mughal Empire, known for administrative reform and religious tolerance.

figures.name: Mohandas (Mahatma) Gandhi
figures.blurb: Apostle of non-violent resistance who led the mass struggle for Indian independence.

sources.label: Republic of India — Republic Day
sources.publisher: Know India, National Portal of India
